section_title: INTRODUCTION TO TIME AND PRICE THEORY (VOLUME 4)
content:
Welcome to Volume 4 of the Inner Circle Trader Market Maker series. This session will focus on Time and
Price Theory, applied to recent market conditions, specifically the British Pound versus US Dollar (Pound
Dollar) hourly chart this week.
- This video is a continuation of parts one, two, and three, which are prerequisites.
- The market context includes SMT divergences, cleared daily sell stops, and an active market maker buy
model.
- The analysis expects a run above relative equal highs and the original consolidation in the market maker
buy model.
- We will now incorporate the elements of time and price into this analysis.

section_title: CHART SETUP AND WEEKLY RANGE EXPECTATION
content:
To ensure consistent understanding, it is crucial to set your chart to New York local time. Daily dividers are
placed at midnight New York time, delineating each day of trading (Monday through Friday). This allows for
viewing the market through the algorithm's lens.
- The week of July 26-30, 2021, for Pound Dollar was bullish.
- Each day represents approximately one-fifth of the weekly range and exhibits repeating characteristics.
- For a bullish weekly range, there is a 70% chance that the low of the week is going to form on Monday,
Tuesday, or by Wednesday's New York session.
- Before directionally driven price movements, opposing stop losses are typically taken out, and then price
will be allowed to deliver in your expected direction.
- In layman's terms, if you're bullish, look for swing lows to get traded below, and then it starts to move
higher. Short term low trades below it, then it starts to trade higher.

section_title: DAILY CHARACTERISTICS AND TRADING THE OPEN
content:
Each day of this week was predominantly bullish until we get to Friday. Looking at an open high low close
perspective of the week, we see the week opening here, a small little movement lower creating the low of
the week on Monday, a retracement and a buying opportunity on Tuesday, a retracement buying
opportunity on Wednesday, then Thursday creating a short-term high of the week, and Friday going just
above the Thursday high before trading off.
- We want to be buying on Monday, Tuesday, and in the best case scenario, if we missed buying
opportunities on Monday and Tuesday, we want to try to buy on Wednesday in the New York session.
- The opening price each day at midnight (New York time) is key. If you're bullish, you want to be buying at
or below, or very close to, the opening price.
- Monday: Price trades below the opening price and rallies up.
- Tuesday: Price opens and trades down through London, then rallies higher on the day.
- Wednesday: Price initially starts to go higher, but then trades down below the open, followed by a
whipsaw below the low on FOMC, then trades higher.
- For your notes: Do not incorporate the opening price on Thursday and Friday for buying influence in a
bullish expected week. These days tend to create the opposite end of the weekly range. Try not to be
looking to be a buyer on Thursday or Friday with the weekly range expectation, as this protects you by
avoiding buying into a market move that has already been moving.
- You can create a model where you get in on a Tuesday after you've seen Monday prove it's wanting to go
higher, and then price trades down below some short-term low, picks up the stops, and provides an optimal
trade entry. You can be a buyer there and allow money to trade without you.
- As a new student, a day like Tuesday, with its decline, might seem 'heart stopping'. But it is going down to
pick up the sell stops, flooding the market with sell stops, allowing smart money to buy those sell stops,
and then it takes off and goes higher.

section_title: HOURLY CHART BREAKDOWN AND SMART MONEY ACCUMULATION
content:
Each day this week, between Monday and Wednesday, offered a buying opportunity to get us above our
target for the weekly range, which was trading above the original consolidation for the buy stops.
- On Tuesday's hourly chart, we see price sweeping below a short term low and rallying.
- On FOMC Wednesday at 2:00 PM New York time, the market whips down, knocking out traders who
would have already trailed their stop loss right below that low. It gets tagged, and then it runs aggressively
higher above that original consolidation (red line), which housed our buy stops.
- Ultimately, price created a short-term high on Thursday, went slightly higher on Friday, and then the profit
- taking model kicked in, returning price back to that original consolidation.
- Study the relationship of the opening price at midnight in New York time and how buying below that
opening price is ideal:
- This is where smart money accumulates longs on Monday.
- This is where smart money accumulates longs on Tuesday.
- This is where smart money accumulates longs on Wednesday.
- They distribute their longs on Thursday and Friday if it offers higher prices, and then you expect price to
come off that and trade back down into the range, which is the highest high formed intra-week to the low.

section_title: DAY OF WEEK AND TIME OF DAY ON 15-MINUTE CHART
content:
On a 15-minute time frame, we incorporate day of week and time of day.
- Monday: The opening price at midnight sees the market trade down, creating the low in London, rallies,
comes back down for an optimal trade entry (OTE). It then trades back down again, taking out a short-term
low during New York open, which represents short-term stops being taken out, followed by an OTE rallying
in New York and continuing higher.
- Most of the time, Monday's trading is left to others because establishing a long there and seeing it rally up
may lead to trailing a stop-loss that gets taken out on Tuesday, requiring repositioning. It is advisable to
study Monday's action and make Tuesday your action day.
- Tuesday: Price is dropped down into London, creating the low of the day by taking out the previous day's
short-term low. Looking at the low to high, you're getting optimal trade entry there.
- The key is OTE or Optimal Trade Entry, which is associated with time of day and kill zones.
- Kill Zones (New York time):
- London Kill Zone: 2 AM to 5 AM
- New York Kill Zone: 7 AM to 10 AM
- New York Session Time (not Kill Zone): 8:30 AM to 11 AM

section_title: THE CRUCIAL ROLE OF TIME AND KILL ZONES
content:
Each turning point that occurs at these kill zones sets up a really nice run in price; for example, a short
- term low is traded below, and then price takes off.
- The element of time, which is associated with the day of the week, is important. Day of the week is a
calendar reference point, a block of time.
- The element of time and price is crucial because your analysis needs to be rooted on the first element,
which is time.
- The markets will not generally give you setups that pan out between these kill zone windows; there will be
a quiet little period, often consolidation, referred to as 'dead time'.
- We're looking to engage if we're bullish, as taught in this series for the Pound Dollar. Monday creates the
low of the week, Tuesday creates a buying opportunity, and New York on Wednesday offers a nice buying
opportunity (though requiring a wide stop).
- Reminder: You should not be trying to trade FOMC if you're a developing student because it's too much of
a whipsaw. You will get caught up with the short-term volatility and be more paralyzed by fear (even with a
demo account) and confused. It is better to simply study it from the sidelines and not engage it at all.
- The element of time is the first crucial factor to reading price, especially when it gets to specific levels.
